attributes.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with a 1-month history of right wrist and hand pain. The pain is now severe enough to cause her difficulty using her right thumb and prevents her from picking up her 6-month-old child. She is otherwise well and has no significant medical history or medication use. On examination she points to the area of pain being localised over the radial styloid process, where there is mild swelling but no bruising. Pain is elicited on resisted abduction of the thumb, as well as a special test performed in which the thumb is enclosed within a fist and passively rotated into ulnar deviation. The neurovascular examination of her right hand is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This woman has De Quervain's tenosynovitis. In addition to a clinical history of radial sided wrist pain worse on moderately heavy lifting, this diagnosis is made through the observation of a positive Finkelstein test, where pain is elicited by pulling the thumb with longitudinal traction across the palm towards the ulnar side. The pain is caused by thickening of the sheath encapsulating the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons, causing entrapment of these tendons. Patients can present with localised pain with or without swelling, and the pain is worsened with movement or grasping objects. One key factor in the stem to take note is the pain when repetitively lifting a child up, which requires thumb extension and abduction that might predispose the patient to developing this condition.

Carpal tunnel syndrome usually presents with numbness and tingling/burning sensations in the radial three and a half digits in the palmar region, and is typically worse at night. Motor deficits and muscle wasting are seen in more advanced disease. Tinel sign and Phalen sign may also be present on examination which are more specific indicators. There would not be localised pain and swelling at the radial styloid process.

Polymyalgia rheumatica is the least likely answer as it usually presents with systemic symptoms in addition to myalgia affecting the shoulders, neck, and pelvic girdle. It most commonly affects elderly women above 50 years old and is associated with morning stiffness more than 45 minutes as well as proximal muscle weakness. These symptoms are not evident in this case.

Rheumatoid arthritis is a systemic disease that presents with polyarthralgia, most likely affecting the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joint and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints of the hand, in addition to the wrist and knee joints. Although abduction of the thumb might cause pain in the MCP joint of the thumb, Finkelstein test does not place stress on the MCP or PIP joints.

Flexor tenosynovitis is an infection in that proliferates in the tendon sheath of a finger, usually originating from a superficial wound and causing localised pain, swelling, and erythema. Due to the inflammation, patients typically present with a finger in fixed flexion with severe pain when trying to extend the finger. Systemic symptoms might arise in severe disease. Even though this condition affects the tendon sheath like De Quervain's, there is no history of trauma and the patient's pain is elicited on testing of the extensor compartment of the wrist.
learningPoints: Finkelstein test - Pain elicited on longitudinal traction of the thumb across the palm towards the ulnar side, positive in De Quervain's tenosynovitis
references: Ilyas AM, Ast M, Schaffer AA, Thoder J. De quervain tenosynovitis of the wrist. J Am Acad Orthop Surg. 2007 Dec;15(12):757-64. doi: 10.5435/00124635-200712000-00009. Erratum in: J Am Acad Orthop Surg. 2008 Feb;16(2):35A. Ilyas, Asif [corrected to Ilyas, Asif M]. PMID: 18063716. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18063716/

Dawson C, Mudgal CS. Staged description of the Finkelstein test. J Hand Surg Am. 2010 Sep;35(9):1513-5. doi: 10.1016/j.jhsa.2010.05.022. Epub 2010 Aug 14. PMID: 20709467. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20709467/
Satteson E, Tannan SC. De Quervain Tenosynovitis. 2023 Nov 22. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan–. PMID: 28723034.

Fakoya AO, Tarzian M, Sabater EL, Burgos DM, Maldonado Marty GI. De Quervain's Disease: A Discourse on Etiology, Diagnosis, and Treatment. Cureus. 2023 Apr 24;15(4):e38079. doi: 10.7759/cureus.38079. PMID: 37252462; PMCID: PMC10208847.
